name: JS & React drills (EIB, Holidu)
description: Spoken-interview versions of the EIB and Holidu quiz topics: this-binding, React data flow and memoization, script loading, keys and controlled inputs.

topics.name: this & function binding
topics.description: How this is determined: call-site, methods, arrow functions, bind.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The rules deciding what the keyword this refers to inside a JavaScript function — which, unlike most languages, is determined by HOW the function is CALLED, not where it was written.

## What problem does it solve?

Nothing — it CAUSES problems, which is why interviews love it. The same function can have a different this on every call, and callbacks silently lose their object.

## How it works

One sentence carries the whole topic: this is decided at the CALL SITE. The cases:
- obj.fn() — method call: this is the object before the dot.
- fn() — plain call: this is the global object; undefined in strict mode.
- new Fn() — constructor: this is the newly created instance.
- fn.call(x) / fn.apply(x) / fn.bind(x) — explicitly set this to x.

Arrow functions are the deliberate exception: they have NO own this. They capture it from the surrounding scope at the moment they are DEFINED, and keep it forever. That is precisely why arrows are the natural choice for callbacks — the this you meant travels with them.

The classic bug: passing a method as a callback detaches it from its object. onClick={obj.handle} eventually calls handle as a plain function — this is undefined, and this.setState explodes. Three standard fixes:
- Bind it: this.handle = this.handle.bind(this) in the constructor.
- Class-field arrow: handle = () => {...} — captures the instance.
- Wrap at the call site: onClick={() => obj.handle()}.

## Key terms

- Call site — where a function is called; what determines this
- Method vs plain call — object before the dot, or undefined/global
- bind/call/apply — setting this explicitly
- Arrow function — no own this; inherits from where it was written
- Lost this — the callback bug and its three fixes

topics.name: React data flow
topics.description: Passing data through the tree, avoiding prop drilling, memoization semantics.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The techniques for getting data to components deep in the React tree — composition, Context — and what the memoization hooks actually promise.

## What problem does it solve?

Prop drilling: a value needed five levels down gets threaded through four components that only pass it along. Noise, coupling, and every middle layer re-renders on change.

## How it works

Reach for tools in this order:
- Composition first: pass COMPONENTS as children instead of data through layers. If the layout component receives {children} already assembled, the middle layers never see the data at all. This solves more drilling than people expect and costs nothing.
- Context second, for genuinely app-wide values: theme, language, the logged-in user. A Provider makes the value available to any descendant that asks.

Context's performance catch: EVERY consumer re-renders whenever the context VALUE changes. Two standard mistakes: passing a fresh object literal as value (new reference every render → everyone re-renders every time — memoize it), and one giant context holding everything (any change wakes every consumer — split by domain: AuthContext, ThemeContext).

What useMemo actually guarantees — less than most people think: it recomputes when dependencies change, and React reserves the right to THROW THE CACHE AWAY whenever it wants. It is a performance hint, not a semantic guarantee. Never put correctness or side effects behind useMemo; if a value must be stable for correctness, that is state or a ref.

## Key terms

- Prop drilling — props passing through components that don't use them
- Composition / children — often the drilling fix with zero machinery
- Context value identity — memoize it, or every render notifies everyone
- Split contexts — by domain, so changes wake fewer consumers
- useMemo — a hint the cache MAY survive, not a promise

topics.name: Script loading (async/defer)
topics.description: How script attributes affect parsing, execution order and page load.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

The script tag's async and defer attributes, which control WHEN JavaScript downloads and runs relative to the HTML being parsed.

## What problem does it solve?

A bare <script src="..."> BLOCKS the page: HTML parsing stops, the script downloads, executes, and only then does parsing continue. Put that in <head> and users stare at a blank page while your bundle downloads.

## How it works

Both attributes download the script in parallel with parsing — they differ in when it EXECUTES:
- async: runs the moment the download finishes, wherever the parser happens to be. Multiple async scripts run in whatever order they arrive — order is NOT guaranteed.
- defer: waits until the document is fully parsed, then runs — and multiple deferred scripts run in DOCUMENT ORDER.

The decision is then mechanical:
- Analytics, ads, anything independent of the page and of other scripts → async: arrive-and-run, order irrelevant.
- Your application bundle → defer: it needs the DOM to exist, and if split across files it needs their order preserved.

Worth adding: <script type="module"> defers by default, and DOMContentLoaded fires after deferred scripts finish — so defer + DOMContentLoaded ordering questions answer themselves.

## Key terms

- Parser-blocking — the default script behavior; the thing to avoid
- async — run on arrival, order not guaranteed
- defer — run after parsing, in document order
- type="module" — modern scripts, deferred by default

topics.name: React correctness basics
topics.description: Keys in lists, controlled inputs, effect timing and shallow-compare pitfalls.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Three small React topics that separate people who know the rules from people who have been bitten: list keys, controlled inputs, and effect timing.

## What problem does it solve?

These three account for a huge share of everyday React bugs: list state mysteriously jumping between rows, inputs fighting the user, effects firing twice.

## How it works

Keys: when rendering a list, key tells React which item is WHICH across renders, so it can move DOM nodes instead of rebuilding them. Using the array INDEX as key breaks on reorder/insert/delete: item state, focus and animations stay glued to the POSITION while the data moves underneath. Use a stable per-item id.

Controlled inputs: an input is controlled when REACT owns its value — value={state} plus onChange updating that state. The input always shows what state says; validation and formatting become trivial. Uncontrolled means the DOM owns the value (defaultValue + a ref to read it when needed). Both are fine; MIXING them is not — a value that flips between undefined and a string triggers the famous "changing an uncontrolled input to controlled" warning and real bugs.

Effect timing: useEffect(fn, []) runs once after the first render — and in development under StrictMode it deliberately runs TWICE (mount → cleanup → mount) to expose effects that are not safe to re-run. That is a feature: if the double-run breaks something, the effect lacks cleanup or idempotence. The cleanup function runs on unmount. Effects never run during render — always after the DOM is updated.

## Key terms

- key — stable identity per item; index breaks on reorder
- Controlled vs uncontrolled — React owns the value vs the DOM does
- StrictMode double-invoke — dev-only, exposes unsafe effects
- Cleanup function — the return value of an effect; runs before re-run and at unmount

questions.title: How `this` works in JavaScript
questions.prompt: Explain how `this` is determined in JavaScript. How do regular functions, methods and arrow functions differ?
questions.answerStructureHint: Call-site rules, method calls, standalone calls (global/undefined in strict mode), arrow functions capturing lexically, bind/call/apply.

expectedPoints.label: this depends on the call site
expectedPoints.description: Method call → the object before the dot; standalone call → global object, or undefined in strict mode.

expectedPoints.label: Arrow functions capture this lexically
expectedPoints.description: Arrows have no own this — they inherit from the enclosing scope, which is why they suit callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Explicit binding
expectedPoints.description: bind/call/apply set this explicitly; losing this when passing methods as callbacks.

followUps.prompt: A class method passed as an onClick handler logs undefined for this. What are your options?

questions.title: Avoiding prop drilling, and what useMemo guarantees
questions.prompt: How do you pass data deep down a React component tree without prop drilling — and what does useMemo actually guarantee?
questions.answerStructureHint: Context and composition (children) against drilling; useMemo as a performance hint recomputing on dependency change — not a semantic guarantee.

expectedPoints.label: Context and composition
expectedPoints.description: React context for app-wide values; component composition (children/slots) often removes the need to drill.

expectedPoints.label: useMemo is a hint, not a contract
expectedPoints.description: Recomputes when dependencies change; React may discard the cache — never rely on it for correctness or side effects.

followUps.prompt: When does putting a value into context hurt performance, and what's the fix?

questions.title: async vs defer script loading
questions.prompt: What is the difference between async and defer on a script tag, and when do you use each?
questions.answerStructureHint: Both download without blocking the parser; async executes on arrival (any order), defer executes after parsing in order.

expectedPoints.label: Both download without blocking
expectedPoints.description: A plain script blocks HTML parsing; async/defer fetch in parallel with parsing.

expectedPoints.label: Execution timing and order
expectedPoints.description: async runs as soon as it arrives (order not guaranteed); defer runs after the document is parsed, in order.

followUps.prompt: Which would you pick for an analytics snippet, and which for your app bundle — and why?

questions.title: Keys, controlled inputs and effect timing
questions.prompt: Three React correctness checks: why do list keys matter, what makes an input controlled, and when does a useEffect with an empty dependency array run?
questions.answerStructureHint: Stable identity for reconciliation (index pitfall), value+onChange makes React the source of truth, mount-effect runs after first render with cleanup on unmount.

expectedPoints.label: Keys give list items stable identity
expectedPoints.description: Reconciliation matches items by key; array indexes break state and animations when the list reorders.

expectedPoints.label: Controlled input = React owns the value
expectedPoints.description: value bound to state plus onChange; uncontrolled uses the DOM with refs/defaultValue.
acceptedSignals: state is the source of truth

expectedPoints.label: Empty-deps effect timing
expectedPoints.description: Runs once after the first render (twice in StrictMode dev), cleanup on unmount — not during render.

followUps.prompt: Why does React StrictMode intentionally double-invoke effects in development?
